title: Construction of Quantum Rank-Metric Codes Using Hermitian Orthogonality
abstract: Stacked quantum memory is an architecture in which multiple layers of qubits are stacked. Quantum rank-metric codes are effective for error correction in stacked quantum memories. However, the previously proposed quantum Gabidulin codes based on the CSS construction had a problem: due to algebraic constraints, the applicable memory layouts were strictly limited to square shapes of odd length. In this paper, we first propose a framework for constructing quantum rank-metric codes from classical linear codes with symplectic self-orthogonality. Building upon this, we propose a new construction method for quantum Gabidulin codes by combining the Hermitian self-orthogonality of classical Gabidulin codes--utilizing the self-dual basis that exists when the extension degree of the finite field is even--with the quantum code construction method using Hermitian orthogonality by Matsumoto and Uyematsu. The proposed method succeeds in approximately doubling the ratio of the minimum rank distance to the number of physical qubits while maintaining the code rate. Furthermore, it eliminates the restriction of the conventional method that requires the number of cells and layers of the stacked memory to be odd, realizing the construction of quantum rank-metric codes applicable to memories with an even number of cells and layers. This construction improves the relative error correction capability of the stacked quantum memory architecture and increases the degree of freedom in design while preserving the code rate.
summary: This paper introduces a new way to build error-correcting codes for stacked quantum memory architectures. By using Hermitian orthogonality instead of the traditional CSS construction, the authors remove previous geometric restrictions and significantly improve the error-correcting efficiency of the system.
main_problem: Existing quantum Gabidulin codes for stacked quantum memories are restricted to square-shaped layouts with an odd number of cells due to algebraic constraints in the CSS construction.
main_result: The authors propose a new construction method using Hermitian orthogonality that eliminates the odd-length restriction and approximately doubles the ratio of minimum rank distance to the number of physical qubits.
method: The method combines the Hermitian self-orthogonality of classical Gabidulin codes with the Matsumoto-Uyematsu mapping to transform classical codes into symplectic self-orthogonal quantum stabilizer groups.
model_or_system: The paper models a stacked quantum memory architecture consisting of multiple layers of qubits arranged in an m x n configuration, where errors are modeled as 'stacked errors' occurring during Clifford circuit execution.
key_observables: Code rate, minimum rank distance, and relative rank distance.
important_parameters: Number of physical qubits (N), number of logical qubits (K), minimum rank distance (d_R), and the extension degree of the finite field.
main_assumptions: The error model focuses on Pauli errors in Clifford circuits, and the construction assumes the existence of a self-dual basis for the finite field extension.
figures_summary: Figure 1 provides a conceptual diagram of a stacked quantum memory with a specific layer and cell configuration.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of existing code limitations, defines the stacked memory error model, proposes a new construction framework using Hermitian orthogonality, provides mathematical proofs for rank invariance and error bounds, and concludes with a comparative analysis and concrete construction example.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Conceptual diagram of a stacked quantum memory (m = 5, n = 4).
